name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl with assorted vegetables, beef, gochujang sauce, and a fried egg.
instructions: Marinate sliced beef in soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic for 15 minutes.
Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil and salt.
Sauté julienned carrot, zucchini, and mushrooms separately with a touch of oil and salt.
Cook marinated beef in a hot pan until browned.
Assemble: hot rice in a bowl, arrange vegetables and beef in sections on top.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up, place on top. Serve with gochujang and sesame seeds. Mix before eating.
tips: Each vegetable is cooked separately to preserve its individual flavor and texture.
For dolsot bibimbap, use a hot stone bowl to get crispy rice at the bottom.

name: Bulgogi
description: Korean marinated beef grilled or pan-fried with pear, soy, garlic, and sesame.
instructions: Slice beef very thinly against the grain (partially freeze for easier slicing).
Blend pear, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and onion into a marinade.
Marinate beef for at least 1 hour (up to overnight).
Cook in a very hot pan or on a grill in batches until caramelized, about 2 minutes per side.
Garnish with sliced spring onions and sesame seeds.
Serve with steamed rice and lettuce wraps.
tips: The pear is a natural meat tenderizer — do not skip it.
Do not crowd the pan — caramelization requires high heat and space.

name: Kimchi Jjigae
description: Spicy Korean kimchi stew with pork belly, tofu, and vegetables simmered in a rich broth.
instructions: Cut pork belly into bite-sized pieces. Sauté in a pot with sesame oil until fat renders.
Add chopped kimchi and its juice, stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, and garlic; stir 1 minute.
Pour in water or stock, add sliced onion, bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 15 minutes. Add cubed tofu, simmer 5 more minutes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion. Serve bubbling hot with rice.
tips: Use well-aged, sour kimchi — fresh kimchi is too mild for jjigae.
The stew should be served still bubbling in the pot.

name: Japchae
description: Korean glass noodles stir-fried with vegetables, beef, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
instructions: Cook glass noodles according to package, drain, cut into manageable lengths, toss with 1 tbsp sesame oil and 1 tbsp soy sauce.
Sauté marinated beef (soy sauce, sugar, garlic) until browned; set aside.
Sauté each vegetable separately with a pinch of salt: carrot, onion, mushrooms, bell pepper, spinach.
Make a thin egg crepe, slice into strips.
Combine everything in a large bowl, add remaining soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil.
Toss gently, garnish with sesame seeds and egg strips. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately is key to authentic japchae.
Glass noodles are best cut with scissors after cooking.

name: Tteokbokki
description: Chewy Korean rice cakes in a sweet and spicy gochujang sauce with fish cakes.
instructions: If rice cakes are frozen, soak in water 15 minutes to soften.
Bring anchovy stock to a boil with gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, and garlic.
Add rice cakes, cook 8-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until cakes are soft and sauce thickens.
Add sliced fish cakes and halved hard-boiled eggs, cook 3 more minutes.
The sauce should be thick and coat the rice cakes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and serve.
tips: Stir frequently to prevent the rice cakes from sticking to the bottom.
The sauce thickens as the rice cake starch releases — add water if it gets too thick.

name: Korean Fried Chicken
description: Double-fried crispy chicken coated in a sweet-spicy gochujang glaze.
instructions: Pat wings dry. Coat with a mix of cornstarch, flour, salt, and pepper.
Fry at 160C for 8 minutes until cooked but pale. Drain and rest 10 minutes.
Fry again at 180C for 5 minutes until deeply golden and extra crispy.
Make sauce: cook gochujang, soy sauce, honey, garlic, ginger, and rice vinegar until thick.
Toss fried chicken in the sauce, drizzle sesame oil.
Garnish with sesame seeds and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the secret to Korean fried chicken — the rest between fries lets moisture escape from the crust.
Toss in sauce just before serving — sitting in sauce makes the coating soggy.

name: Sundubu Jjigae
description: Spicy Korean soft tofu stew with seafood, vegetables, and a cracked egg.
instructions: Heat sesame oil in a small pot, sauté diced onion and garlic.
Add gochugaru and gochujang, stir 30 seconds.
Pour in anchovy stock, bring to a boil. Add sliced zucchini.
Add clams and shrimp, cook 3 minutes.
Gently slide in silken tofu in large spoonfuls (do not stir too much).
Simmer 5 minutes, crack an egg on top, cover for 1 minute. Serve bubbling with rice.
tips: Silken tofu is extremely delicate — add it gently and do not stir vigorously.
The stew should be served still bubbling in its pot.

name: Dakgalbi
description: Spicy Korean stir-fried chicken with vegetables, sweet potato, and rice cakes in a gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Mix gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and ginger into a marinade.
Marinate chicken for 30 minutes.
Slice sweet potato, cabbage, and onion. Soak rice cakes if using dried.
Stir-fry chicken in sesame oil for 5 minutes.
Add sweet potato and rice cakes, cook 5 minutes with a splash of water.
Add cabbage and onion, stir-fry 5 minutes until everything is cooked. Garnish with perilla leaves.
tips: Dakgalbi is traditionally cooked on a large flat pan at the table.
Add mozzarella cheese in the center for a popular modern variation.

name: Samgyeopsal (Grilled Pork Belly)
description: Thick-cut pork belly grilled at the table, wrapped in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and kimchi.
instructions: Slice pork belly into 1cm thick pieces.
Grill on a hot pan or grill plate until crispy and golden on both sides, about 4 minutes per side.
Slice raw garlic cloves. Set up lettuce, perilla leaves, ssamjang, kimchi, and garlic at the table.
Cut cooked pork into bite-sized pieces with scissors.
To eat: place pork in a lettuce leaf, add ssamjang, garlic, and kimchi, wrap and eat in one bite.
Dip in sesame oil mixed with salt and pepper for an alternative.
tips: Cut the pork thick — thin slices dry out on the grill.
The lettuce wrap should be eaten in one bite — Korean ssam tradition.

name: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: Stir-fried rice with aged kimchi, pork, gochujang, and a fried egg on top.
instructions: Dice pork belly, fry in a wok until fat renders and meat is crispy.
Add chopped kimchi and its juice, stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add rice, gochujang, soy sauce, and sesame oil; stir-fry on high heat 3 minutes.
Make a well in the center, crack eggs in and scramble, then mix into rice.
Alternatively, fry eggs separately and place on top.
Garnish with spring onion, sesame seeds, and seaweed flakes.
tips: The kimchi must be well-fermented and sour for the best flavor.
Day-old rice is essential — fresh rice becomes mushy.

name: Doenjang Jjigae
description: Korean fermented soybean paste stew with tofu, zucchini, and vegetables.
instructions: Bring anchovy stock to a boil. Add doenjang paste and stir to dissolve.
Add diced potato, cook 5 minutes until half-tender.
Add diced zucchini, onion, and minced garlic; simmer 5 minutes.
Add cubed tofu and sliced green chili, simmer 5 more minutes.
Sprinkle gochugaru and sliced spring onion.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
tips: Doenjang is intensely savory — a little goes a long way.
This is Korean comfort food at its best — simple but deeply flavorful.

name: Gimbap
description: Korean seaweed rice rolls filled with vegetables, egg, and pickled radish.
instructions: Season warm rice with sesame oil, salt, and sesame seeds.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil. Julienne carrot and sauté briefly.
Make thin egg crepes, roll and slice into strips.
Place seaweed on a bamboo mat, spread rice thinly leaving 2cm at the top edge.
Arrange fillings in a line: spinach, carrot, egg, cucumber, pickled radish.
Roll tightly using the mat, brush outside with sesame oil, slice into 2cm rounds.
tips: Do not overfill — a tight roll is better than a fat, sloppy one.
Wetting the knife between cuts gives clean slices.

name: Galbi (Korean BBQ Short Ribs)
description: Sweet soy-marinated beef short ribs grilled until caramelized and tender.
instructions: Score the meat between bones if using flanken cut.
Blend pear, onion, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sugar, mirin, sesame oil, and pepper into a marinade.
Marinate ribs for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Grill over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until caramelized.
Cut between bones into individual pieces.
Garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion, serve with rice and ssamjang.
tips: The pear tenderizes the meat naturally — do not skip it.
High heat and a quick cook give the best result — do not overcook.

name: Jajangmyeon
description: Korean-Chinese noodles in a thick black bean sauce with pork and vegetables.
instructions: Dice all vegetables and pork into 1cm cubes.
Heat oil, fry chunjang paste for 2 minutes to remove raw taste.
Add diced pork, cook until browned.
Add onion, potato, zucchini, and cabbage; stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add 300ml water and sugar, simmer 10 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry. Cook noodles, drain, top with sauce and julienned cucumber.
tips: Frying the black bean paste first removes its raw, bitter edge.
Jajangmyeon should be mixed thoroughly before eating.

name: Haemul Pajeon (Korean Seafood Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean pancake loaded with spring onions, shrimp, squid, and a soy dipping sauce.
instructions: Mix flour, rice flour, egg, ice water, and salt into a thin batter.
Cut spring onions to the length of the pan. Chop shrimp and squid.
Heat oil in a large flat pan. Pour in batter, spread thinly.
Arrange spring onions and seafood on top, press gently into batter.
Cook on medium-high for 3-4 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy.
Flip carefully, cook 3 minutes more. Make dipping sauce: soy sauce, vinegar, sesame oil. Serve hot.
tips: Ice water in the batter makes it extra crispy — similar to tempura technique.
Press the pancake down firmly while cooking for maximum crunch.
